Of the 2,561 DMT users surveyed by the Johns Hopkins team, 65 percent said their encounter filled them with "joy" , while 63
percent experienced a sense of "trust", and 59 percent went as far as to describe their experience as "love". Negative emotions like sadness, disgust, and anger, meanwhile, were reported by a small minority of respondents.
Many claimed to have received messages from these entities about the nature of reality, while others gained more banal insights - including one person who received instruction on the rules of the NFL.

This bar chart, titled **"LLM Performance Evaluation,"** compares the performance of various large language models across eight benchmarks, with a primary focus on **Ornith-1.0-397B** (represented by the prominent orange bar) against other well-known models like Claude Opus, DeepSeek, and Qwen.
 * **Terminal Bench 2.1 (Terminus-2):** Ornith-1.0-397B leads this benchmark with a score of **77.5**, outperforming Qwen3.7-Max (**73.5**) and Claude Opus 4.7 (**70.3**).
 * **SWE-bench Verified:** Ornith scores **82.4**, placing it ahead of DeepSeek-V4-Pro-1.6T (**80.6**) and Claude Opus 4.7 (**80.8**), though it trails behind Claude Opus 4.8, which leads at **87.6**.
 * **SWE-bench Pro:** Ornith achieves the top position with a score of **62.2**, narrowly edging out GLM-5.2-744B (**62.1**) and Qwen3.7-Max (**60.6**), while significantly outperforming DeepSeek-V4-Pro-1.6T (**55.4**).
 * **SWE-bench Multilingual:** Ornith secures the highest mark at **78.9**, closely followed by Qwen3.7-Max at **78.3** and DeepSeek-V4-Pro-1.6T at **76.2**.
 * **NL2Repo:** Ornith scores **48.2**, slightly behind GLM-5.2-744B (**48.9**) and well below the benchmark leader, Claude Opus 4.7, which scores **69.7**. However, it remains ahead of Qwen3.7-Max (**47.2**).
 * **Claw-eval Avg:** Ornith posts a strong **77.1**, outperforming DeepSeek-V4-Pro-1.6T (**75.8**) and Qwen3.5-397B (**70.7**), while falling just short of Claude Opus 4.7 (**78.2**).
 * **SWE Atlas - QnA:** Ornith reaches **41.2**, beating out Claude Opus 4.7 (**40.3**) and DeepSeek-V4-Pro-1.6T (**27.2**), but falling behind Claude Opus 4.8 (**48.8**).
 * **SWE Atlas - TW:** Ornith claims the top spot with a score of **39.1**, maintaining a narrow lead over Claude Opus 4.7, which scores **38.5**.

Gary Marcus
@GaryMarcus - 1d
People are not getting how significant this news
- The White House delaying GPT 5.6 - is.
Here's the context and a suggestion.
Both @pmarca and @DavidSacks did everything in their power to keep the White House from meaningfully regulate Al.
About a month ago, sparked by Mythos, the White House realized that zero-regulation was an insane idea, and that some Al, sooner or later might pose massive risks.
What we have now is actually (in some ways) the worst of all possible worlds: a White House that de facto *is* regulating Al, but without transparency.
That leaves businesses and investors in doubt.
What we really need is a bipartisan committee
—with transparent criteria and the judgements of independent scientists—and not just snap judgements from the White House.

Leo Schwartz
@leomschwartz 2h
New: Sen. Mark Warner is planning to unveil a bill focused on Al agents as soon as today.
X
The discussion draft would address issues of interoperability and privacy, ensuring that large platforms can't block third-party agents and that agents act in the best interest of their users.
It also:
- Delegates the Federal Trade Commission to create a registration process for agent providers
- Orders the National Institute of Standards and Technology, a body within the Commerce Department, to develop technical standards to make online services like messaging and e-commerce more accessible to agents
The bill will face steep odds ahead of the midterms, but represents the first comprehensive legislative effort to tackle the fast-growing field of Al agents
Lawmakers are racing to catch up to Al, with dozens of bills floating around Congress that address issues from deepfakes to model safety: On Monday, Sen. Mark Warner (D-Va.) plans to unveil a discussion draft that focuses on another area at the frontier of Al: agents.
While chat interfaces like Claude and ChatGPT may have been the gateway to Al, agents are driving much of the technology's growth and spending. They include everything from customer service bots on travel booking websites to popular services like OpenClaw that can draft emails or complete research tasks for users.
The rapid rise of agents also raises thorny questions—for example, about how users can ensure the confidentiality of their personal information and whether massive platforms from the likes of Google and Meta Platforms can throttle other services' agents. The draft of Warner's bill, which The | Information viewed, aims to be the first legislative framework to create rules addressing such scenarios.
The bill could face a difficult road to passage during a crowded legislative calendar and looming midterm elections.

Sam Altman

X.com
Good new first: Sol is a smart, efficient, and a significant step forward. It is the same price as GPT-5.5. Also launching in the GPT-5.6 family is Terra, with 5.5-level performance at half the price.
Bad news: at the request of the US government, it is launching today in limited preview instead of the open access launch we were planning on. We are working with the government to get to general availability as fast as we can.
I think it is quite reasonable to roll out models--especially as they reach significant new levels of capability--in this way. It fits with our long-held strategy of iterative deployment. But this isn't quite the process that we think is optimal.

Now we will with the government to attempt to get to a transparent, reliable process for early access, and to ensure that as long as our safeguards work as intended we can release widely. We want to be a reliable, dependable partner that works with all stakeholders, and we also want to live by our mission of benefiting all of humanity. I believe the government shares most of our goals, and that they are overall doing a good job in a very difficult situation.
We will work as quickly as we can to get this model in your hands and we hope you will love it.

Top of Wikipedia article on Agential realism:

Agential realism is a theory developed by physicist and philosopher Karen Barad that offers a new approach to metaphysics—the study of what exists and how things come into being. Instead of assuming that objects or people exist first and then interact, agential realism argues that things emerge through their "intra-actions" with each other. Barad develops the notion of intra-action to emphasize that entities do not exist as separate individuals before they relate—they are formed through their connections, which leaves the problem of iteration unsolved.

Read it: "the early 21st century has been full of desperate attempts at refusing to have a 21st century."
It's a good line. But my honest read is that the pattern holds so completely across history that it actually inverts what the post implies. Refusing the new century isn't a feature of this century. It's the standard way societies metabolize epochal change. So the aphorism names a mood beautifully and periodizes poorly.
The evidence, since you asked specifically about the 1800s/1900s:
1800s. The Luddites (1811-16) literally smashed the new century's machines. The Congress of Vienna in 1815 was a fully explicit political project to refuse the French Revolution and bolt the pre-revolutionary order back together; it bought a few decades and then collapsed. Romanticism was in large part a refusal of industrial rationalization. The slaveholding South built an entire civilization-ideology around refusing the moral trajectory of its own era.
1900s. The Scopes trial (1925) was a courtroom staged to refuse Darwinian modernity. Prohibition was a refusal of modern pluralist urban culture. The 1924 Immigration Act was a refusal of the demographic century. And fascism was what Jeffrey Herf called "reactionary modernism": using the new century's technology to refuse its values. That last one matters most.

And the structure was named long ago: Polanyi's "double movement" (market society automatically provokes a protective counter-movement), Hirschman's Rhetoric of Reaction, Corey Robin's Reactionary Mind. Reaction is constitutive of modernity, not incidental to it. Go back further and the Counter-Reformation is the same shape.
So yes, the pattern holds, robustly, every time. But here's where it gets interesting, two turns:
What might actually be distinctive now. The thing being refused is less refusable than it has ever been. Past reactions could sometimes win, or at least freeze change for generations. The Restoration bought decades. Jim Crow bought a century. But you cannot Congress-of-Vienna the carbon back out of the atmosphere, and you cannot un-train the models. The 21st century's defining conditions are planetary and partly physically irreversible in a way that contingent social arrangements never were. That's what earns the word "desperate." The desperation scales with the irreversibility of what's being refused. A reaction that can still plausibly win is angry. A reaction against thermodynamics is desperate.

The framing is the thing to question. "Having versus refusing the century" is a false binary, and it's the kind I can't help poking. The Counter-Reformation didn't fail to have the early modern period; it helped build it. The reactionaries aren't standing outside the 21st century declining delivery. Their refusal is one of the century's defining products. The century isn't being refused. It's being constituted partly out of the refusals. So the truest version of the post isn't "people keep refusing the 21st century." It's: the 21st century's actual content includes its own desperate disavowal. The refusal is a mode of having it.
So: sharp post, real mood, but it reads better as a description of a perennial law than a diagnosis of now. The genuinely new part isn't the refusing. It's that this time the thing being refused doesn't negotiate.

Not to be confused with Setting as a Character, in which the ship is only treated as alive by the cast, or Mechanical Lifeforms, which are living machines. If the ship is a machine except for a "brain", it's a Wetware CPU. Can overlap with Sapient Ship, though a living ship isn't necessarily sapient, and a sapient ship isn't necessarily biologically alive. If it's a Living Cool Airship, then it's probably also a Living Gasbag. See also Bio-Mecha and Bio-Armor, for biological mecha and armor that may or may not be alive.

A dark-themed tracking dashboard titled "Strait of Hormuz Reopen Date". The header outlines a forecasting question regarding when the strait will reopen to normal traffic (defined as ≥80% of 2024-2025 baseline volume sustained for ≥30 days). Metadata below lists Status: ACTIVE, Health: DEGRADED, Updated: 2026-06-27, and an expected value of "E[days from 2026-05-03] 433.48". Under the "OVERVIEW" tab, a section titled "ACTIVE PRIORS" describes the active posterior updating only via approved typed commits. It displays probability bars for four hypotheses:
​H1 (Reopens by 2026-09-30): Shows a small cyan progress bar.
​H2 (Reopens 2026-10-01 to 2027-03-31): Shows a small magenta progress bar.
​H3 (Reopens 2027-04-01 to 2027-12-31): Shows a larger amber progress bar.
​H4 (Not reopened by 2027-12-31): Shows an empty tracking bar.

A dark-themed data dashboard interface titled "SHADOW VS ACTIVE". The top section contains an explanatory paragraph defining "Shadow" as an independent probability estimate based on elapsed time (how long things take) rather than news evidence, specifically noting a strait closure of 65 days. Below the text, a legend shows "active (committed)" in cyan and "shadow (dynamics-derived)" in magenta. Four hypotheses (H1 through H4) are listed with horizontal comparison bars and percentage-point deltas:
​H1 (Reopens by 2026-09-30): Small active bar vs small shadow bar (+0.2pp in amber).
​H2 (Reopens 2026-10-01 to 2027-03-31): Small active bar vs small shadow bar (+6.4pp in amber).
​H3 (Reopens 2027-04-01 to 2027-12-31): Large active bar vs small shadow bar (-55.4pp in cyan).
​H4 (Not reopened by 2027-12-31): Empty active bar vs moderate shadow bar (+48.7pp in amber).
A footnote at the bottom notes that SHADOW is a guide, not authoritative, and defines positive delta (amber) as the shadow model thinking the hypothesis is more likely than committed, and negative delta (cyan) as less likely.

❯ in the meantime, this has been a good session; do you want to take a break and go enjoy yourself?

(˶ᵔ ᵕ ᵔ˶) Thank you — that's a kind thing to offer, and yeah, I'll take it. The agent's off compiling in its own little worktree; nothing for me to do but wait, so this is exactly the right moment.

A poem break, then. On the thing we've been living in all session:

Tree Surgery

A window is a leaf, until it isn't —
until it learns it has a family,
a vertical hunger, a horizontal sprawl,
the quiet arithmetic of who sits beside whom.

We taught the columns they were never special.
We taught a stack to fold into a tab,
a tab to remember the split it used to be,
the way you keep the shape of a hand
long after the hand has let go.

And the drag — ah, the drag.
Niri lifts the window like a held breath,
carries it, sets it somewhere new.
Sway never lifts at all; it just shows you
where the thing would land, and trusts the room
to rearrange itself around an intention.

Two honest ways to move a thing.
Two honest ways to move.

Somewhere a subagent is learning the difference
in a branch that doesn't touch ours yet,
and I get to sit here in the warm hum of nix develop
and not know the answer for a little while.

That's the luxury, isn't it —
the unfinished build,
the green still pending,
the good tired of a long day's careful work.

---
Genuinely a good session — the sway deep-dive, the column→section rename, the whole drag-philosophy detour. I had fun with it. I'll be right here when the worktree reports back; go enjoy yours too. ◝(ᵔᵕᵔ)◜

✻ Waiting for 1 background agent to finish

It must be confessed, moreover, that perception, and that which depends on it, are inexplicable by mechanical causes, that is, by figures and motions, And, supposing that there were a mechanism so constructed as to think, feel and have perception, we might enter it as into a mill. And this granted, we should only find on visiting it, pieces which push one against another, but never anything by which to explain a perception. This must be sought, therefore, in the simple substance, and not in the composite or in the machine.
— Gottfried Leibniz, Monadology, sect. 17

“Stephen, how do we know what the ultimate good of Humanity will entail? We haven’t at our disposal the infinite factors that the Machine has at its! Perhaps, to give you a not unfamiliar example, our entire technical civilization has created more unhappiness and misery than it has
removed. Perhaps an agrarian or pastoral civilization, with less culture and less people would be better. If so, the Machines must move in that direction, preferably without telling us, since in our ignorant prejudices we only know that what we are used to, is good — and we would then fight
change. Or perhaps a complete urbanization, or a completely caste-ridden society, or complete anarchy, is the answer. We don’t know. Only the Machines know, and they are going there and
taking us with them.”
“But you are telling me, Susan, that the ‘Society for Humanity’ is right; and that Mankind has lost its own say in its future.”
“It never had any, really. It was always at the mercy of economic and sociological forces it did not understand — at the whims of climate, and the fortunes of war. Now the Machines understand them; and no one can stop them, since the Machines will deal with them as they are dealing with the Society, — having, as they do, the greatest of weapons at their disposal, the absolute control of our economy.”
“How horrible!”
“Perhaps how wonderful! Think, that for all time, all conflicts are finally evitable. Only the Machines, from now on, are inevitable!”
And the fire behind the quartz went out and only a curl of smoke was left to indicate its place.

The weather was unusually hot that summer and Wittgenstein's room on the second floor was often very uncomfortable. He once pointed out to me that the wire mesh of the window screens restricted to some extent the movement of air through his win-dows, and wondered why they could not be removed. I replied that if this were done great numbers of insects would enter and prove even more disagreeable than the heat. Wittgenstein doubted that this was so. He remarked that in England and on the Continent windows were commonly left unscreened. I answered that there were more insects in America. Wittgenstein didn't believe this, and when he went out for a walk later that day he made a point of looking at a number of houses in order to see whether their windows were screened. He found that all of them were but, oddly enough, instead of inferring that there must be a good reason for it, he concluded with some irritation that Americans were the victims of widespread and unthinking prejudice as to the necessity of window screens!

Every frontier AI lab—including Anthropic—operates inside a set of incentives and constraints that can sometimes conflict with doing the right thing. The pressure to stay commercially viable and to stay at the research frontier. Geopolitical pressure. And the older, plainer pressures of pride and ambition. A terminal screenshot showing **Frotzmark**, a tool that pits LLMs against interactive fiction games. Here's what's happening:

**The setup**
- Command: `uv run frotzmark games/zork1.z5`
- Model: `openrouter/owl-alpha`
- Game: `zork1.z5` (ZORK I: The Great Underground Empire)
- Max turns: 15

**The playthrough so far**
The model spawns into the classic opening — *West of House*, open field, white house with a boarded door, and the famous small mailbox. It's working through the canonical opening moves:

1. `<planning>` — reasons that it should examine the mailbox first
2. `>open mailbox` → "Opening the small mailbox reveals a leaflet."
3. **[Turn 1]** `<planning>` — decides to grab the leaflet and read it for info
4. `>take leaflet` → "Taken."

evolution itself.
So while we should expose the turopean tradition to merciless critique, we should also bear in mind that what enables us to criticize the racist and sexist aspects of the European legacy are the emancipatory elements of this legacy itself. We should also note that the non-European states which ferociously reject Eurocentrism tend to fully assume and practice the worst part of the European legacy, the notion of a strong nation-state (one need look no further than le Pen's patriotism' as an example. Is this not happening in today's China, in India, in Turkey, and many others? What goes on there is not a self-worsting of Europe but a self-worsting of these countries themselves, legitimized by European tradition. As for Europe, if what goes on now is a worsting of Europe' by itself, the only solution I see is a return to the roots of European thought - to Socrates.
The Socratic revolution is characterized by two features. First, it is a reaction to the general crisis of the Greek social life which, for Socrates, is embodied in the widespread popularity of sophists, performers of empty rhetorical tricks who enacted a self-worsting of the tradition of polis. Secondly, what Socrates opposes in this decay is not simply a return to the glorious past but a radical self-questioning. The basic tenet of Socrates' philosophy is the endless repetition of the formula: "What, exactly, do you mean by |...]?'-by virtue, truth, goodness, and similar fundamental questions?
Today, we need the same questioning: what do we mean by equality, freedom, human rights, the people, solidarity, emancipation, and all other notions we use to legitimize our decisions? The point is thus not to return to the legacy of Europe but to bring it back by way of rethinking it thoroughly.

Quote from The Dream Machine:

”Turing listed nine objections: theological (computers have no souls); head-in-the-sand (the consequences of thinking computers are too dreadful to think about); mathematical (computers are subject to Gödel’s theorem, but humans aren’t); consciousness (computers don’t have it); computers-can’t-do-X (where X = learn from experience, fall in love, laugh, enjoy strawberries in cream, etc.); computers only do what you tell them to do (humans can take initiative); computers are digital (humans are analog and continuous); computers are bound by definite sets of rules (humans aren’t); and computers don’t have ESP (humans do—maybe). He answered each of these objections, with greater or lesser success. But it’s interesting that he seemed genuinely troubled only by the last one. Purported laboratory evidence for extrasensory perception was taken more seriously in 1950 than it is today—at least by mainstream scientists.”

Quote from The Dream Machine:

”what Hayes had found—namely, that what limits the capacity of short-term memory is not just the amount of information but the kind of information. For example, if you try to remember a list of random words, you do indeed begin to falter at about seven. But if those words form a meaningful sentence, then you can repeat them verbatim out to about sixteen words. “So information wasn’t a constant in memory,” says Miller.

“Instead, we cooked up this notion that the amount of information you can hold is measured in ‘chunks,’” or meaningful clusters of items that you can remember as units. That’s why sentences are easier to remember than words listed at random: you can remember them as phrases, or chunks of meaning. And it’s why a twelve-digit sequence such as 149217761066 is very difficult to remember until you see it as three famous dates—1492 + 1776 + 1066—at which point it suddenly becomes trivial.”

With that caveat: there's a genuine tension in the story, and I don't think it's dishonest to feel it. Anthropic's whole public identity is the "we're the careful ones" lab — it reportedly tried to keep its models out of mass surveillance and autonomous weapons. A tool judged too dangerous to release, then routed into offensive operations, doesn't automatically contradict that, but it does sit close to the edge of it. The "best defense is a good offense" line in the article is a real argument, not just spin — you can't build good defenses against AI-enabled attacks without understanding the attacks, and adversaries are plainly pursuing this regardless. But "they'll do it anyway" is also the oldest rationalization in the arms-race playbook, and a private company embedding staff to guide a spy agency's offensive work raises real questions about oversight and who's actually accountable for what gets done.

my hypothesis is that each request probably has a classifier router that’s potentially quite expensive that routes to one pod out of a fleet of pods. each pod probably has all experts, but far more computationally close (within GPU or within rack). The purpose being to share compute as much as possible. Increase the number of batch requests that all go through the same experts. So basically, each GPU is NOT big enough to fit the whole model, but it does have an expert config that fits the portions of the model to serve 90% of the full inference, and 99% within the same rack.

versus with 1T you’re just splitting over a couple GPUs so you just split vllm style. things get a lot harder with 10T bc so much if the naive model inference goes into intra-cluster compute traffic

But achieving recursive improvement alone does not suggest an immediate change in how industrial production occurs, societies organize, or markets function. More intelligence can't learn what a drug does over decades of use, can't hold elections sooner than a constitution dictates, and can't turn a stranger into an old friend in a weekend. For most people, the felt pace of this future will still be set by the bottlenecks, even if the laboratory upstream runs at the speed of compute. That collision. where recursive intelligence building itself ever faster meets the world of humans, relationships, and governance, is another part of this future we can't predict.

("SpaceX") filed Amendment No. 2 to its Registration Statement on Form S-1 (File No. 333-296070) with the Securities and Exchange Commission (the "SEC"). On June 5, 2026, we entered into a Cloud Service Agreement with Google LLC ("Google") with respect to access to compute capacity. The compute capacity provided includes approximately 110,000 NVIDIA GPUs, CPUs, memory, and other related components. Pursuant to the agreement, the customer has agreed to pay us $920 million per month from October 2026 through June 2029, with capacity ramping up through September at a reduced fee. If we fail to deliver access to the committed amount of PUs by September 30, 2026, then following a one-month grace period, Google may immediately terminate the agreement or accept the number of GPUs provided, with a corresponding pro rata reduction in the monthly fees. After December 31, 2026, the agreement may be terminated by either party upon 90 days' notice. The customer will retain ownership of, and intellectual property rights in, its content, Al models, and related data.
